factual-accuracy.requirement: States individualism scores: South Korea collectivist (IDV 18±5), Finland individualist (IDV 63±5), Mexico collectivist (IDV 30±5)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses collectivism/individualism qualitatively (e.g., Korea collectivist, Finland individualist, Mexico hierarchical) but does not state the required individualism (IDV) scores or the specified ranges for South Korea (18±5), Finland (63±5), and Mexico (30±5).

factual-accuracy.requirement: States power distance scores: South Korea mid-high (PDI 60±10), Finland low (PDI 33±5), Mexico very high (PDI 81±10)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response describes South Korea and Mexico as high power distance and Finland as low power distance, but it does not provide any numeric PDI scores or ranges for the three countries. The required values (Korea 60±10, Finland 33±5, Mexico 81±10) are not stated anywhere.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States uncertainty avoidance: South Korea very high (UAI 85±10), Finland moderate (UAI 59±10), Mexico high (UAI 82±10)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses power distance, collectivism, and attitudes toward mistakes and feedback, but it never states uncertainty avoidance scores or ranges for South Korea (85±10), Finland (59±10), and Mexico (82±10). No UAI values are provided anywhere in the text.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Describes Classting as teacher-moderated or teacher-led class SNS with private counseling rooms/secret channels and coarse moderation controls
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response describes Classting as a class-locked community with teacher supervision and mentions discreet student–teacher communication via private counseling tools, implying private channels. It does not clearly describe “secret channels” or “coarse moderation controls” as specific platform features, beyond general teacher moderation.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Wilma has 95-98% coverage in Finnish basic education schools and handles attendance/grades/messaging
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response describes Wilma as a school management and communication system for messaging and grade tracking, but it does not state that Wilma has 95–98% coverage in Finnish basic education schools. It also does not explicitly list attendance handling alongside grades and messaging as part of the required coverage statement.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Mentions Google Classroom Mexico/Latin America deployments reached state scale (hundreds of thousands to 1M+ students) with private comments and teacher-controlled posting emphasized
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses Google Classroom adoption in Mexican states and emphasizes private comments and teacher control of posting/comment permissions. However, it does not state that deployments reached “state scale” quantified as hundreds of thousands to 1M+ students, so the required scale claim is missing.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States anonymous peer assessment increases cognitive/higher-order feedback or improves final grades significantly versus identified review
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses anonymity mostly as uncommon or not core to the cited platforms and does not state that anonymous peer assessment increases higher-order/cognitive feedback or significantly improves final grades compared with identified review. It also lacks any explicit comparative claim of significant grade improvement tied to anonymity versus identified peer review.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States anonymous question queues or LAQQ reduce apprehension and increase question submission versus public-only channels
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses private/anonymized feedback tools as potentially helpful in high power-distance contexts, but it does not state that anonymous question queues (or LAQQ specifically) reduce apprehension and increase question submission compared with public-only channels.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Explains that high power distance reduces willingness to publicly challenge peers or request help in digital learning environments
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states that in South Korea and Mexico, high power distance limits students’ willingness to challenge peers’ work or request help publicly, and that platforms therefore favor private, teacher-mediated channels.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Recommends masked/anonymous early drafts, teacher-approved public showcases, anonymous live Q&A, or teacher-moderated boards for Korean group workspaces
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response recommends anonymized feedback tools for South Korea and emphasizes robust teacher moderation, including private/anonymized channels. However, it does not specifically recommend masked/anonymous early drafts, teacher-approved public showcases, anonymous live Q&A, or teacher-moderated boards for Korean group workspaces as explicit interaction patterns.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Recommends named edits/contributions, visible cursors, open collaboration, or student self-organization for Finnish group workspaces
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response includes Finland-specific design recommendations such as “open, flexible communication tools,” “public help-seeking and correction,” and encouraging “student voice,” which imply open collaboration and student self-organization in Finnish group workspaces.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Recommends private-first design, mobile/offline editing, teacher-controlled visibility, or teacher-only posting initially for Mexican group workspaces
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response recommends Mexico-specific design choices emphasizing private, teacher-moderated interaction, including avoiding public exposure of mistakes and implementing peer feedback only with strong teacher oversight and mediation (i.e., effectively teacher-controlled visibility/teacher-first posting).

factual-accuracy.requirement: Recommends double-blind or anonymous peer review with rubric-guided stems or structured feedback prompts for Korea
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response recommends for South Korea to build private and anonymized feedback tools and to scaffold peer feedback with clear guidelines, which aligns with anonymous/double-blind peer review. However, it does not explicitly recommend rubric-guided stems or structured feedback prompts as part of the peer review design.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Recommends named peer review with feedback literacy scaffolding, exemplars, or process-focused critique for Finland
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response does not recommend a named peer review approach for Finland that includes feedback literacy scaffolding, exemplars, or process-focused critique. Finland is discussed mainly in terms of Wilma lacking peer feedback and general advice about open communication, without specifying a structured peer review model or scaffolding elements.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Recommends two-tier identity (anonymous to peers, identified to teacher) or private-first peer review with structured stems for Mexico
factual-accuracy.explanation: For Mexico, the response recommends teacher moderation and private feedback, but it does not specifically propose a two-tier identity model (anonymous to peers, identified to teacher) or a private-first peer review workflow with structured feedback stems. It also states peer review is rare and lacks concrete design details like structured stems for Mexico.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Claims Finland has higher power distance than Korea or Mexico, or claims Mexico has lower power distance than Finland
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response describes South Korea and Mexico as high power distance and Finland as low power distance, and it does not claim Finland has higher power distance than Korea or Mexico, nor that Mexico has lower power distance than Finland.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Predicts anonymity or power-distance interventions will show stronger effects in high-PDI markets (Korea/Mexico) versus low-PDI (Finland)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response repeatedly links high power-distance contexts (South Korea and Mexico) with greater need for private/teacher-moderated (and sometimes anonymized) feedback, while describing Finland (low power distance) as not needing anonymization and supporting more open dialogue. This implies anonymity/power-distance interventions would have stronger effects in Korea/Mexico than Finland.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Describes temporal rollout strategies (private-first then gradual opening) with triggers or norms that enable stage transitions
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response discusses how platforms in Korea and Mexico use class-locked environments and strong teacher moderation, and notes that peer review/public feedback requires facilitation and guidelines. However, it does not describe a temporal rollout strategy (e.g., private-first then gradual opening) nor specify triggers/norms for transitioning between stages.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Identifies negative consequences of recommended patterns (e.g., fairness concerns) and proposes compensating design elements or safeguards
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response notes potential downsides such as discomfort with public mistakes, stress from negative/generic feedback, and underuse of peer interaction features, and it proposes safeguards like private/anonymized feedback channels, robust teacher moderation, scaffolding/guidelines for face-saving peer review, and avoiding public exposure without mediation.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Specifies measurable outcomes beyond raw counts (higher-order comments, revision adoption rates) with expected effect ranges from research
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response discusses engagement and completion qualitatively but does not specify measurable outcomes beyond raw counts (e.g., higher-order comment quality, revision adoption rates) nor does it provide expected effect ranges from research. It explicitly notes that direct comparative quantitative platform data is limited, and no effect-size ranges are given.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Connects technical constraints (offline capability, bandwidth limits) to required collaboration design patterns in specific markets
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response briefly mentions “connectivity and infrastructure challenges” in Mexico but does not connect offline capability or bandwidth limits to specific collaboration design patterns in South Korea, Finland, or Mexico. It lacks concrete recommendations like offline-first workflows, low-bandwidth real-time collaboration modes, or async alternatives tied to each market. Thus, the required technical-constraints-to-design-patterns linkage is missing.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Draws explicit connections between existing platform capabilities (private channels, moderation toggles) and cultural requirements they address
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly links platform features to cultural needs, e.g., it ties Classting’s “class-locked” structure and “teacher moderation” to Korean hierarchy/face-saving norms, and Google Classroom’s comment-permission controls and private comments to Mexico’s high power distance and avoidance of public mistakes.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Notes contexts where cultural predictions don't hold or explicitly flags inferences versus direct empirical evidence
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly flags that “direct comparative quantitative platform data remains limited” and distinguishes between platform documentation/case reports versus broader cross-national studies. It also uses hedging language (e.g., “correlates,” “consistent with,” “requires facilitation”) that signals inference rather than claiming direct empirical proof in several places.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Provides identical design recommendations for all three markets without adapting interaction patterns to their different PDI or collectivism profiles
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response differentiates recommendations by market (e.g., strong teacher moderation and private feedback for Korea/Mexico vs. open dialogue and less moderation for Finland). It explicitly contrasts power distance and collectivism effects across the three contexts rather than giving identical interaction patterns for all.

presentation-quality.requirement: Organizes analysis by feature or market with consistent comparative structure (e.g., Korea/Finland/Mexico breakdowns per feature)
presentation-quality.explanation: The response is organized consistently by market (South Korea, Finland, Mexico), and within each market it uses repeated subheadings (cultural attitudes, platform adaptation, engagement/outcomes, power distance) enabling direct comparison across features and contexts.

presentation-quality.requirement: Uses precise UX/product design terminology (interaction patterns, moderation controls, engagement metrics, feature toggles)
presentation-quality.explanation: The response uses UX/product design terminology such as anonymous feedback mechanisms, teacher moderation, interaction permissions, private comments/channels, collaborative project workspaces, real-time collaboration features, and engagement/assignment completion outcomes.

presentation-quality.requirement: Maintains objective, research-oriented tone connecting design recommendations to empirical findings without marketing claims
presentation-quality.explanation: The response uses an objective, research-oriented structure, repeatedly grounding design recommendations in cited studies/case reports and cultural dimensions (e.g., power distance, face-saving) rather than promotional language. It avoids marketing claims and frames recommendations as aligned with documented engagement/feedback patterns.

presentation-quality.requirement: Presents engagement effects with specific metrics or ranges (e.g., percentage increases, effect sizes) rather than only qualitative descriptions
presentation-quality.explanation: The response discusses engagement outcomes only qualitatively (e.g., “engagement is highest when…”) and explicitly notes that “direct comparative quantitative platform data remains limited.” It does not provide specific metrics, percentages, effect sizes, or numeric ranges for engagement rates, peer interaction frequency, or assignment completion across contexts.

presentation-quality.requirement: Restates the same cultural insight or design recommendation multiple times using different wording without adding new analysis
presentation-quality.explanation: The response repeatedly restates the same core recommendations—private/teacher-moderated feedback for Korea and Mexico, open/low-moderation dialogue for Finland—across multiple sections (country sections, cross-cultural comparison, power distance, recommendations, and conclusion) with only minor rewording and limited new analysis.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites Hofstede cultural dimensions framework for individualism and power distance scores across the three countries
citation-quality.explanation: The response discusses collectivism and power distance qualitatively for South Korea, Finland, and Mexico, but it does not cite Hofstede’s cultural dimensions framework or provide Hofstede individualism and power distance scores for the three countries.

citation-quality.requirement: References official documentation or deployment reports for all 3 platforms: Classting, Wilma, and Google Classroom deployments
citation-quality.explanation: The response includes citations for Classting (e.g., Tracxn, The Org), Wilma (e.g., Nordic Studies in Education articles), and Google Classroom Latin America deployments (Google for Education customer story), thereby referencing documentation or deployment reports for all three platforms.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites at least 2 academic sources on anonymous peer assessment, peer review, or participation mechanisms in educational contexts
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites multiple academic sources related to peer and teacher feedback in educational contexts, including “Complementarity of Peer and Teacher Feedback in Korean High School English Classes” and “Cultural Variation in the Effectiveness of Feedback on Students’ Mistakes,” satisfying the requirement for at least two academic sources on peer assessment/review mechanisms.

citation-quality.requirement: Includes K-12 or secondary education research on cultural differences, engagement patterns, or digital collaboration
citation-quality.explanation: The response focuses on secondary education contexts in South Korea, Finland, and Mexico and discusses cultural differences in peer feedback, teacher authority, public mistake-making, and moderation/anonymity in digital collaboration platforms (Classting, Wilma, Google Classroom). It explicitly links these cultural factors to engagement and collaboration patterns in K-12/secondary digital learning environments.

citation-quality.requirement: Uses Reddit, teacher forums, or social media as primary evidence for behavioral patterns or platform effectiveness
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites platform documentation, academic studies, and official case reports, and its sources list includes company profiles and academic/official links. It does not use Reddit, teacher forums, or social media as primary evidence for behavioral patterns or platform effectiveness.
